sources.dataset: BEA Regional Price Parities (state, All items)
sources.note: Cost-of-living index; real value of a constant wage.

method: Selected top workforce-adjusted-burden states; computed a naive absolute-SES index and a workforce-adjusted relative-standing index (real wage value via BEA RPP; wage-to-local-median via ACS; broadband). Min-max normalized within candidates; ranked.
integrity: Relative across all 50 states + DC; never a per-person probability.
Screening heuristic, not a value judgment about under-resourced geographies.
Anchor wage cancels in ranking (documented); OEWS state wages = door-open refinement.
ABM diffusion deliberately out of v1 (door open).

evidence.claim: Voluntary health-program participation skews toward higher-SES, already-healthier employees (selection / 'healthy-participant bias') — and the same selection is why such programs often show small ROI. So 'more engageable' is NOT 'more savings'; validate against real engagement data.
evidence.source: Illinois Workplace Wellness Study, QJE 2019 (RCT: selection + near-null effects)

evidence.claim: Lower-SES employees participate less in, and comply less with, workplace health-promotion programs.
evidence.source: IPD meta-analysis of SES inequalities in workplace health promotion (2020)

evidence.claim: Cost-adjusted pay is grounded in financial bandwidth, not affluence: financial scarcity consumes the cognitive bandwidth needed for non-urgent preventive health — a better-motivated lever than 'higher income engages more'.

limitations: Engageability is a literature-informed SCREENING PRIOR, not a fitted coefficient; strength and even direction vary by program design and population.
'More engageable' ≠ 'more savings' (healthy-participant bias): the most engageable members may need intervention least.
Relative standing is applied to engageability but NOT symmetrically to burden (see Step 3); a joint member-SES → (burden, engageability) model is the rigorous fix (ABM/research).
